followup_notes.changes_from_prior_cycle: Elevated the observer-dependence and entropy-production objections to 'major' and 'moderate' severity respectively, influenced by codex's substantive physics analysis. Score lowered slightly from 5.0 to 4.5 to reflect that codex's physical objections are well-grounded even if the specific equation/figure references they cite may be hallucinated. Verdict unchanged at 'routine' — the paper is PRD-published and the concerns, while real, may be addressed in the body text. Confidence marginally increased from 0.35 to 0.40.
invalid_points_from_other_reviewers: Gemini: Giving 0/10 and 'major_flaw' because the article body is missing from the review bundle conflates a data-collection/scraping failure with a quality defect in the paper itself. The paper is published in Phys. Rev. D after peer review; the missing text is a limitation of the review process, not of the paper. A score of 0 and confidence of 1.0 are unjustified under these circumstances.

Codex: References to specific equation numbers (Eqs. 2–3, 5–8) and figure numbers (Figs. 4–5) are not present in the supplied bundle text and appear to be hallucinated extrapolations from the section-title outline. While the underlying physics concerns are legitimate, the specific in-text citations should be treated with caution.
valid_points_from_other_reviewers: Codex: Observer-dependence of de Sitter thermality is a genuine foundational concern — treating it as a real cosmological relic source without resolving the static vs. comoving observer ambiguity is the central gap in the mechanism (confidence 0.78, substantively well-founded).

Codex: Entropy production at the transition to radiation domination could substantially modify the entropy-normalized yield; the instantaneous-transition idealization may not be self-consistent with a physical reheating model.
Codex: Near-Planck mass claims may fall outside the regime of semiclassical validity for horizon thermality.
Gemini: Correctly identifies that the full article body is missing from the bundle and that reviewing from the abstract alone is severely limited.

main_claims.claim: A post-inflationary quasi–de Sitter phase with -1 < w < -1/3 produces stable massive particles via horizon thermal emission that freeze in and constitute the observed dark matter abundance.
main_claims.notes: The physical setup is coherent with Gibbons–Hawking theory for exact de Sitter. The extension to quasi-de Sitter (w ≠ -1) is non-trivial and the derivation is inaccessible. PRD acceptance provides some credibility that the peer referees were satisfied.

main_claims.claim: The horizon temperature in a quasi–de Sitter Universe is inversely proportional to the proper horizon size (Gibbons–Hawking analogue).
main_claims.notes: The exact Gibbons–Hawking result is firmly established for stationary de Sitter. Its validity for w ≠ -1 is debated (refs 5, 10, 11, 13, 14 in the reference list address these subtleties). Whether the paper's treatment is adequate is unknown without the body text.
main_claims.paper_location: Abstract assumption (2); section title 'OBSERVERS IN DE SITTER AND QUASI–DE…'

main_claims.claim: The dark matter mass range spans ~10 keV to near the Planck scale as a function of w and T_reh.
main_claims.notes: This is a parameter-space mapping rather than a sharp prediction. The upper end (near-Planck masses) is potentially outside the semiclassical regime where the horizon temperature formula is reliable. Codex's concern about near-Planck validity is legitimate.

missing_assumptions.assumption: The range of w (equation-of-state parameter) for the post-inflationary phase is not stated in the abstract — the condition appears truncated in the HTML rendering, likely reading -1 < w < -1/3.
missing_assumptions.why_it_matters: The allowed range of w determines the physical distinctiveness from inflationary production and controls the parameter space of the mass prediction.

missing_assumptions.assumption: No justification in the abstract for why horizon-produced particles are 'frozen in' immediately rather than thermalized by any coupling to the radiation bath.
missing_assumptions.paper_location: Abstract ('does not undergo any number-changing processes')
missing_assumptions.why_it_matters: Even purely gravitational couplings can thermalize particles if the reheating temperature is high enough. The self-consistency of the frozen-in assumption with the required particle content needs demonstration.

missing_assumptions.assumption: The physical mechanism that drives and terminates the post-inflationary quasi–de Sitter phase is not specified.
missing_assumptions.why_it_matters: The model that generates the accelerated phase determines whether it is physically realizable without conflicting with inflationary model building, and constrains the parameter space for (w, T_reh).

missing_assumptions.assumption: An adiabaticity or quasi-stationarity condition for applying the Gibbons–Hawking-type temperature formula throughout the quasi–de Sitter phase is not stated.
missing_assumptions.why_it_matters: Without this condition, the temperature formula may not hold for the full duration of the phase, invalidating the integrated production rate.

needs_human_spotcheck.question: Does the paper rigorously establish that static-patch Gibbons–Hawking thermality in a quasi–de Sitter background sources a frame-independent, observer-independent comoving relic density rather than a detector-dependent effect?
needs_human_spotcheck.why_model_cannot_resolve: This is the central conceptual issue in QFT in curved spacetime. The full derivation in section 'OBSERVERS IN DE SITTER AND QUASI–DE SITTER…' is inaccessible. Resolving it requires reading the actual proof and comparing it against the literature on observer-dependence (e.g., Mottola 1985, Anderson & Mottola 2013/2017).

needs_human_spotcheck.question: Do the quantitative results depend critically on the adiabatic approximation for quasi–de Sitter, and does the paper bound the regime of validity for w values significantly above -1?
needs_human_spotcheck.why_model_cannot_resolve: The derivation in section III is inaccessible. Whether the paper applies the Gibbons–Hawking temperature formula only near w = -1 or across the full -1 < w < -1/3 range, and whether it controls the error, cannot be assessed from the abstract.

needs_human_spotcheck.question: How does the predicted relic abundance quantitatively compare to Garny, Sandora, Sloth (2016) [ref 53] and Chung, Kolb, Riotto (1998) [ref 52]? What is the incremental derivation beyond those papers?
needs_human_spotcheck.why_model_cannot_resolve: The introduction and discussion sections containing this comparison are inaccessible from the bundle.

needs_human_spotcheck.question: Does the paper provide a controlled upper bound on the entropy injected during the transition from the quasi–de Sitter phase to radiation domination, and is the entropy-normalized yield Y = n/s stable against this injection?
needs_human_spotcheck.why_model_cannot_resolve: The transition model and entropy conservation argument are in the body text, which is not available.

novelty_assessment: The mechanism — thermal particle production from a cosmological horizon populating a dark matter relic — is not conceptually new. Parker (1968/69) established gravitational particle production; Gibbons–Hawking (1977) established the de Sitter horizon temperature; Chung, Kolb, Riotto (1998) [ref 52] and Garny, Sandora, Sloth (2016) [ref 53] applied these ideas to superheavy and gravitational dark matter production during inflation. The specific contribution is to focus on a post-inflationary quasi–de Sitter phase with a general equation-of-state parameter w ≠ -1, which shifts the production epoch and allows lighter dark matter masses (down to ~10 keV) than purely inflationary scenarios. If the quasi-de Sitter generalization is carefully justified and self-consistent, this constitutes a meaningful but incremental extension of existing work — not a conceptually new mechanism. The very broad mass range (10 keV to M_Pl) is a limitation of predictivity. Novelty is moderate.
score_rationale: Score is 4.5/10, slightly lower than cycle 1 (5.0), reflecting the codex reviewer's substantive physics concerns about observer-dependence and entropy production that I now weight more heavily as independent confirmation of genuine gaps. The paper is PRD-published by a recognized expert, which sets a credibility floor; the abstract is internally coherent and the mechanism is physically motivated. However: (1) the full text remains inaccessible, so technical soundness cannot be verified; (2) the observer-dependence issue is a known foundational debate in QFT-in-curved-spacetime that the abstract does not resolve; (3) the mass range of 10 keV to M_Pl is so broad as to limit predictivity; (4) the mechanism is incremental over Parker/Gibbons–Hawking/Chung-Kolb-Riotto/Garny-Sandora-Sloth. Gemini's 0/10 verdict is rejected as methodologically invalid (scraping failure ≠ paper flaw). Reviewer confidence remains at 3/10 because we cannot read the body text.
summary: Profumo proposes dark matter production via thermal emission from a cosmic quasi–de Sitter horizon during a post-inflationary accelerated expansion phase, yielding a relic abundance consistent with observations for dark matter masses from ~10 keV to near the Planck scale, depending on equation-of-state parameter w and reheat temperature. The paper is published in Phys. Rev. D (July 2025), authored by a recognized dark matter theorist, and passed PRD peer review. CRITICAL CAVEAT (unchanged from cycle 1): the bundle contains only the APS abstract landing page; the full article body is paywalled and was not scraped. All judgments remain speculative. The codex reviewer appears to reference specific equation numbers (Eqs. 5–8) and figure numbers (Figs. 4–5) that are not present in the supplied bundle text — these references should be treated with caution as potentially hallucinated content extrapolated from the section-title outline. The gemini reviewer gave 0/10 and 'major_flaw' solely because the article body is missing from the bundle, which confuses a data-collection failure with a flaw in the paper itself and is an invalid inference. Codex's substantive physics objections — particularly about observer-dependence of de Sitter thermality and entropy production at the transition — are well-grounded in the literature and represent the most actionable concerns raised in this cycle.

technical_objections.evidence: It is well established in the literature that Gibbons–Hawking thermality in de Sitter is an observer-dependent (static-patch) effect. The codex reviewer flags this concern with 0.78 confidence based on text visible to them. Parker production and Gibbons–Hawking production have different physical interpretations. Whether the paper resolves this for an FLRW relic yield is unknown but is the central conceptual gap.
technical_objections.objection: Observer-dependence of de Sitter thermality is not resolved in the abstract. The static-observer assumption (assumption 3) is an idealization during a dynamically evolving non-stationary background; whether this thermality sources a frame-independent comoving relic density is a foundational QFT-in-curved-spacetime issue.
technical_objections.paper_location: Abstract assumptions (2) and (3); section 'OBSERVERS IN DE SITTER AND QUASI–DE…'

technical_objections.evidence: Refs 5, 9–14 in the bundle address subtleties of particle production in de Sitter and quasi-de Sitter backgrounds, suggesting the authors are aware of this issue, but whether they fully resolve it is unknown without the body text.
technical_objections.objection: The validity of the quasi-de Sitter temperature formula for w not close to -1 requires an adiabaticity or slow-roll condition that is not stated in the abstract. For w significantly above -1, the spacetime deviates substantially from de Sitter and a simple inverse-horizon-size temperature formula may not control particle production.

technical_objections.evidence: This concern is raised by codex with 0.67 confidence and is physically well-motivated: any mechanism that ends the quasi–de Sitter phase and transfers energy to radiation generically produces entropy. The instantaneous-transition approximation commonly used in such calculations can underestimate this effect.
technical_objections.objection: Entropy production at the transition from the quasi–de Sitter phase to radiation domination is not addressed in the abstract. The relic abundance is an entropy-normalized quantity (Y = n/s), so unmodeled entropy injection during reheating could substantially shift the predicted mass range.
technical_objections.paper_location: Abstract ('the temperature when radiation domination starts and the quasi–de Sitter phase ends')

technical_objections.evidence: When the produced particle mass approaches M_Pl, the semiclassical QFT-in-curved-spacetime framework begins to break down. Backreaction corrections are not bounded from the abstract alone.
technical_objections.objection: Near-Planck dark matter masses may fall outside the semiclassical regime where horizon thermal emission and the geometric-optics approximation are reliable. The upper end of the claimed mass range could be uncontrolled.

technical_objections.evidence: Direct inspection of the reference list in the bundle shows these repetitions. This is likely a scraping artifact from the APS HTML page rendering. It does not reflect a flaw in the actual published paper.
technical_objections.objection: The reference list contains apparent duplicates: Gibbons–Hawking is cited three times (refs 2, 27, 37) with a volume discrepancy (D 17 vs D 15 for ref 2); refs 3/31 (Lapedes), 7/30 (Medved), 8/28 (Parikh), and 9/29 (Sekiwa) appear to be exact duplicates.
